---
title: "Simulation for Robotics"
---

Simulation enables robot learning at a scale and speed impossible in the physical world.
A single GPU cluster can run thousands of parallel environments, generating years of
experience in hours. The Physical AI Toolchain builds on NVIDIA Isaac Sim to provide
production-grade simulation infrastructure for reinforcement learning and evaluation.

![Virtual environments enable safe, scalable robot training before real-world deployment.
*Image: [Computer simulation](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Blender3D_HottubAfter.jpg)
by Blender Foundation, [CC BY-SA 3.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/)*
](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Blender3D_HottubAfter.jpg/512px-Blender3D_HottubAfter.jpg){fig-alt="3D rendered simulation environment" width="55%"}

## Why Simulation?

Training a robot policy requires millions of environment interactions. Collecting this data
on physical hardware is impractical for four reasons:

| Motivation | Physical World | Simulation |
|------------|---------------|------------|
| **Safety** | Collisions damage hardware and surroundings | Collisions are free |
| **Scale** | One robot, real-time speed | Thousands of parallel environments, faster than real-time |
| **Cost** | Hardware wear, human supervision | GPU compute only |
| **Repeatability** | Environment varies uncontrollably | Deterministic resets, controlled randomization |

These advantages make simulation the standard first stage in the robot learning pipeline.
The challenge is ensuring that policies trained in simulation transfer to real hardware—the
*sim-to-real gap*.

## Physics Engines

A physics engine computes how objects move and interact. Robotics simulation requires
multiple physics modalities:

**Rigid-body dynamics** models objects as non-deformable solids with mass, inertia, and
contact forces. This covers most manipulation scenarios: grasping boxes, stacking objects,
pushing items on a table.

**Articulated-body dynamics** extends rigid-body simulation to kinematic chains—robot arms,
hands, and legged platforms. Joints introduce constraints that couple the motion of
connected links. The recursive Newton-Euler algorithm and Featherstone's Articulated Body
Algorithm are standard solvers.

**Soft-body and deformable simulation** handles cloth, rubber, and biological tissue. These
require finite element methods (FEM) or position-based dynamics (PBD) and are significantly
more expensive than rigid-body simulation.

**Fluid dynamics** simulates liquids and granular materials using smoothed-particle
hydrodynamics (SPH) or material point methods (MPM). Relevant for food handling, pouring,
and agricultural robotics.

## NVIDIA Isaac Sim Architecture

Isaac Sim is NVIDIA's robotics simulation platform built on three pillars:

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-isaac-arch
%%| fig-cap: "NVIDIA Isaac Sim Architecture Stack"
flowchart TB
    subgraph Omniverse["NVIDIA Omniverse Platform"]
        USD[Universal Scene Description]
        RENDER[RTX Renderer]
    end

    subgraph Physics["Physics Engine"]
        PHYSX[PhysX 5 SDK]
        RIGID[Rigid Body Solver]
        ARTIC[Articulation Solver]
        SOFT[Soft Body / FEM]
        GPU_ACCEL[GPU-Accelerated Pipeline]
    end

    subgraph IsaacLab["Isaac Lab Framework"]
        ENV[Environment Manager]
        OBS[Observation Manager]
        ACT[Action Manager]
        REW[Reward Manager]
        TERM[Termination Manager]
    end

    subgraph RL["RL Training"]
        SKRL_A[SKRL Agent]
        RSL[RSL-RL Agent]
        VEC[Vectorized Environments]
    end

    USD --> PHYSX
    RENDER --> GPU_ACCEL
    PHYSX --> RIGID & ARTIC & SOFT
    GPU_ACCEL --> ENV
    RIGID & ARTIC --> ENV
    ENV --> OBS & ACT & REW & TERM
    OBS & ACT --> VEC
    VEC --> SKRL_A & RSL
```

**Omniverse** provides the scene representation layer using Universal Scene Description (USD),
NVIDIA's adopted extension of Pixar's scene format. USD enables composable, layered scene
authoring—robots, objects, and environments are assembled from reusable components.

**PhysX 5** is the physics engine, fully GPU-accelerated. It solves rigid-body contacts,
articulated-body dynamics, and collision detection on the GPU without CPU round-trips. This
enables thousands of environments to run in parallel on a single GPU.

**RTX rendering** produces photorealistic images via ray tracing and path tracing. For RL
training that uses state observations (joint positions, object poses), rendering is disabled
to maximize simulation throughput. For vision-based policies or synthetic data generation,
RTX rendering provides camera images indistinguishable from real photographs.

## The Simulation Loop

Every training step follows the same cycle:

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-sim-loop
%%| fig-cap: "Simulation Training Loop"
flowchart LR
    RESET[Reset Environments] --> OBS[Observe State]
    OBS --> POLICY[Policy Selects Action]
    POLICY --> STEP[Physics Step]
    STEP --> REWARD[Compute Reward]
    REWARD --> DONE{Episode Done?}
    DONE -->|No| OBS
    DONE -->|Yes| RESET
```

In vectorized training, all environments execute each stage simultaneously. A single call to
`env.step(actions)` advances all $N$ environments in parallel, returning $N$ observations,
$N$ rewards, and $N$ termination flags. This batched execution is what makes GPU-accelerated
simulation orders of magnitude faster than sequential alternatives.

## Sim-to-Real Transfer

A policy trained in simulation encounters discrepancies when deployed on real hardware:

- **Dynamics mismatch**: simulated friction, damping, and contact models are approximations
- **Sensor noise**: real cameras and encoders produce noise absent in clean simulation
- **Actuator lag**: real motors have latency and bandwidth limits not modeled precisely
- **Visual gap**: rendered images differ from real-world appearance

### Domain Randomization

The primary technique for bridging the sim-to-real gap is **domain randomization**: varying
simulation parameters during training so the policy learns to be robust to a range of
conditions that brackets reality.

Common randomization targets:

| Parameter | Randomization Range | Effect |
|-----------|-------------------|--------|
| Object mass | ±30% of nominal | Robustness to payload variation |
| Friction coefficients | 0.3–1.0 | Handling different surfaces |
| Joint damping | ±20% | Compensating for actuator modeling errors |
| Observation noise | Gaussian $\sigma = 0.01$ | Tolerance to sensor imprecision |
| Action delay | 0–3 steps | Robustness to communication latency |
| Gravity vector | ±0.5° tilt | Compensating for mounting imprecision |

By training over a distribution of environments, the policy learns behaviors that work
across the entire range—including the real world, which is (ideally) one sample from that
distribution.

## Photorealistic vs Headless Simulation

Isaac Sim operates in two modes:

**Headless (state-based training)**: rendering is disabled entirely. The environment returns
numerical state vectors—joint positions, object poses, contact forces—directly from the
physics engine. This mode maximizes throughput and is used for all RL training in the
Physical AI Toolchain.

**Rendered (vision-based training and evaluation)**: RTX rendering generates camera images.
This is necessary for training vision-based policies, generating synthetic training data
for perception models, and visual evaluation of learned behaviors. Rendering reduces
environment throughput by approximately 10×.

## Parallel Environments

The performance advantage of GPU simulation is dramatic:

```{python}
#| eval: false
#| code-fold: false
import omni.isaac.lab as lab
from omni.isaac.lab.envs import ManagerBasedRLEnvCfg

# Configuration for parallel environments
env_cfg = ManagerBasedRLEnvCfg(
    num_envs=4096,              # 4096 environments in parallel
    episode_length_s=5.0,       # 5-second episodes
    sim=lab.sim.SimulationCfg(
        dt=0.005,               # 200 Hz physics
        render_interval=0,      # headless mode (no rendering)
        gravity=(0.0, 0.0, -9.81),
        physx=lab.sim.PhysxCfg(
            num_threads=0,      # GPU solver (no CPU threads)
            solver_type=1,      # TGS solver
            num_position_iterations=4,
            num_velocity_iterations=0,
            gpu_found_lost_pairs_capacity=2**21,
            gpu_total_aggregate_pairs_capacity=2**21,
        ),
    ),
)

# Stepping 4096 environments simultaneously
# env = ManagerBasedRLEnv(cfg=env_cfg)
# obs, info = env.reset()
# for step in range(1000):
#     actions = policy(obs)
#     obs, rewards, terminated, truncated, info = env.step(actions)
```

At 4096 environments and 200 Hz physics, a single training step generates 4096 transitions.
A 5-second episode at 200 Hz produces 1000 steps. One pass through all environments yields
~4 million transitions—collected in seconds of wall-clock time on a single GPU.

## Digital Twins

A **digital twin** is a simulation environment that mirrors a specific physical installation
with high fidelity: exact robot model, workspace geometry, sensor placement, and object
properties. Unlike generic simulation environments used for pre-training, digital twins
enable:

- Fine-tuning policies for a specific deployment site
- Validating behavior changes before deploying to physical hardware
- Monitoring real-time state by synchronizing sensor data into the simulation

The toolchain supports digital twin workflows through USD scene composition—real-world
environments are modeled as USD stages that can be loaded into Isaac Sim for both training
and evaluation.

## Robotics Simulator Comparison

| Simulator | Physics Engine | GPU Parallel | Rendering | Language | License |
|-----------|---------------|-------------|-----------|----------|---------|
| **Isaac Sim** | PhysX 5 (GPU) | Thousands of envs | RTX ray tracing | Python | Proprietary (free) |
| **MuJoCo** | Custom (CPU/GPU) | MJX for GPU | OpenGL | Python/C | Apache 2.0 |
| **PyBullet** | Bullet (CPU) | Limited | OpenGL | Python | zlib |
| **Gazebo** | ODE/DART/Bullet | No | OGRE | C++/Python | Apache 2.0 |
| **Drake** | Custom (CPU) | No | VTK | C++/Python | BSD-3 |
| **Brax** | Custom (JAX) | TPU/GPU native | None | Python | Apache 2.0 |

Isaac Sim's combination of GPU-accelerated physics, photorealistic rendering, and massive
parallelism makes it the preferred choice for the Physical AI Toolchain.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Throughput Rule of Thumb
For state-based RL, expect ~50,000 environment steps per second per GPU with 4096
environments on an H100. Vision-based training with 128×128 RGB cameras drops to ~5,000
steps per second. Plan GPU hours accordingly when estimating training job costs.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Isaac Sim Containers on AKS
The Physical AI Toolchain runs Isaac Sim inside NVIDIA containers (`nvcr.io/nvidia/isaac-lab`)
on AKS GPU nodes. Training jobs submitted via AzureML or OSMO pull this container image and
execute headless simulation on H100 or RTX PRO 6000 GPUs. The container requires
`ACCEPT_EULA=Y`, `PRIVACY_CONSENT=Y`, and `NVIDIA_DRIVER_CAPABILITIES=all` environment
variables. PhysX 5 runs entirely on the GPU—no CPU physics threads are used. The Vulkan
graphics API is required even in headless mode for GPU resource management, which is why
`NVIDIA_DRIVER_CAPABILITIES` must include the `graphics` capability (included in `all`).
:::
